ClinicalDocument.title: Continuity of Care Document - Mila Harris

value.displayName: for persistent vomiting to enable oral rehydration
if amoxicillin failure or recent antibiotic use

act.text: "She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and complained her throat felt scratchy. By afternoon she had a fever and just wanted to lay around, which isn't like her. We gave her ibuprofen and she's been drinking water okay, but she's not interested in eating much. This morning her temperature was 101.3, so we thought we should get her checked out to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: History and exam findings consistent with viral URI: fever (101.3°F), clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, and mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate support upper respiratory viral infection; clear lungs exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 440mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 290mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 11 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

Mila got bit by something—we think a mosquito—on her leg yesterday afternoon while playing outside. This morning she woke up with a big red, swollen bump that's been itching like crazy, and now there are a few more smaller bites on her arms. We put some hydrocortisone cream on it last night and gave her Benadryl, but the swelling hasn't really gone down and she keeps scratching it raw. We're mainly worried it's going to get infected if she keeps picking at it.

Insect Bite Reaction: Clinical presentation of localized erythema and edema at bite site with normal vital signs (HR 81) and unremarkable systemic exam (clear lungs, normal HEENT) supports benign insect bite reaction without signs of systemic involvement or secondary infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

Mom reports that Mila woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been complaining of a sore throat. She's been pretty tired and doesn't want to eat much, though she's drinking okay. They gave her ibuprofen yesterday which brought the fever down temporarily, but it came back up this morning to 101.5°F. Mom is mainly concerned because Mila has a soccer tournament this weekend and wants to make sure it's nothing serious.

Fever: Clinical presentation consistent w/ fever: Elevated HR (98) suggests physiologic response to elevated core temp; HEENT and lung exam unremarkable, making localized infection less likely and supporting nonspecific viral etiology.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and sore throat. By afternoon she was complaining her whole body ached and had a bad cough. We gave her ibuprofen and some fluids, but she's been pretty miserable—won't eat much and just wants to sleep. We're worried because her fever keeps coming back even after the medicine, and she's coughing so much she can barely sleep at night."

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent w/ influenza: Pt presents w/ classic viral URI sx in context of known flu exposure/prevalence. Vital signs stable (HR 88) w/ benign exam (HEENT nl, lungs clear bilaterally) supports uncomplicated influenza w/o secondary bacterial infection or pneumonia.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a sore throat and has had a fever on and off since then—it got up to 101.5 this morning. She's been complaining that her head hurts and she just seems really tired, won't eat much even though I made her favorite lunch. We gave her Tylenol a few times and that helped a little, but I'm worried because she's never been sick like this before and I want to make sure it's not something serious."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: normocephalic, atraumatic HEENT exam r/o trauma/meningismus; clear lungs bilaterally r/o pneumonia; normal HR appropriate for age. No focal findings to suggest bacterial infection or complications.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 10 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 9 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI: Mila woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been complaining of a sore throat. She's been pretty tired and won't eat much, though she's been drinking okay. We gave her ibuprofen yesterday which brought the fever down for a few hours, but it came back up to 102 this morning. We're mostly worried because she's never had a fever this high before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious.

Fever: Clinical presentation of fever with normal vital signs (HR 95), clear lungs, and benign HEENT exam suggests likely viral etiology without evidence of acute respiratory infection or meningitis at this time. Further workup warranted to identify source if fever persists or additional symptoms develop.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen
Well-child examination - 8 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI (Parent Narrative): Mila woke up yesterday morning complaining that her ear hurt and felt like it was plugged. She's had a fever since then—we checked it this morning and it was over 101. She's been pretty miserable, not wanting to eat much and just wanting to lay around, and last night she kept tugging at her ear. We gave her some ibuprofen and tried a warm compress, which helped a little, but she's still uncomfortable so we thought we should get her checked out.

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media (AOM): erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy indicate middle ear inflammation and effusion, supported by fever and systemic signs.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1850mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1850mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 200mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and sore throat. By yesterday afternoon she was complaining that her head hurt and she just wanted to lay on the couch—she wouldn't eat much. We gave her some ibuprofen and she seemed a little better, but this morning the fever came back and she's still not acting like herself. I'm mainly worried because she's never been sick like this before and I want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: normal vital signs (HR 80), unremarkable HEENT exam, and clear bilateral lung fields argue against bacterial infection or respiratory complications. Absence of focal findings supports self-limited viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She got bit by something yesterday afternoon when she was playing outside. At first it was just a small red bump on her leg, but this morning it got really swollen and puffy—like twice the size—and she's been scratching at it constantly even though I keep telling her not to. We put some hydrocortisone cream on it last night and gave her some Benadryl this morning, but it doesn't seem to be helping much. I'm worried it's getting infected or that she's going to make it worse by scratching."

Insect Bite Reaction: Clinical presentation consistent with insect bite reaction: localized inflammatory response to arthropod exposure with normal vital signs (HR 78) and unremarkable systemic exam (HEENT normocephalic, lungs CTAB), ruling out systemic allergic reaction or secondary infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up this morning with a really stuffy nose and has been sneezing a lot all day. Her eyes are itchy too and she keeps rubbing them. We gave her some children's Benadryl this afternoon and that helped a little bit, but the sniffling won't stop. I'm worried it might turn into an ear infection or something worse since she's been congested before."

Allergic Rhinitis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ allergic rhinitis: HEENT exam without acute infection findings (normal head, no trauma), clear lungs bilaterally r/o lower respiratory involvement, and HR 103 likely reflects age-appropriate response to URI-like sx rather than systemic infection.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 7 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 6 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever - I took her temperature and it was 102.5. She's been really fussy and complaining that her head hurts, and she won't eat much, just wants to drink. I gave her some ibuprofen this morning and it came down a bit, but it keeps going back up. I'm mainly worried because she's never had a fever this high before, and she's just not acting like herself."

Fever: History and exam consistent w/ fever: Tachycardia (HR 105) supports systemic inflammatory response; normal HEENT and clear lungs help exclude localized infection (otitis media, pneumonia) as primary etiology, suggesting possible viral illness or early undifferentiated febrile illness requiring further workup.
- [medication] Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for temperature control - [education] Monitor for source of infection, return if fever persists >3 days - [follow-up] Follow up as needed if symptoms worsen

"Mila woke up yesterday morning pulling at her right ear and complaining it hurt. She's had a runny nose for about a week from a cold, but yesterday the ear pain got really bad. She's been fussy, won't eat much, and I noticed she felt warm so I took her temperature this morning and it was almost 101. I gave her some ibuprofen and it helped a little bit, but she's still tugging at her ear and seems uncomfortable, so I wanted to get her checked out."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings consistent with AOM: erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy in febrile child (100.9°F) with intact lungs r/o concurrent respiratory infection.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1500mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1500mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 170mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 5 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started throwing up yesterday afternoon, maybe around 2 o'clock, and it's happened about five or six times since then. This morning she had diarrhea too, and she's been running a fever—we checked it a couple times and it's been around 101. She's not interested in eating or drinking much, just wants to lay around, which isn't like her at all. We gave her some ginger ale and crackers but she threw that up, so we're worried she's getting dehydrated and wondering if we need to do anything else."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: fever and tachycardia (HR 120) suggest systemic viral infection, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternative diagnoses (otitis media, pneumonia). Vitals and exam findings compatible w/ uncomplicated viral gastroenteritis.

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been coughing on and off all day. This morning she had a fever—I took her temperature and it was 101.5. She's been pretty tired and doesn't want to eat much, just wants to drink juice and lay around. I gave her some children's ibuprofen this morning and it helped a little, but I'm worried the fever will come back. She's never had anything like this before, so I wanted to get her checked out." --- *This narrative reflects typical parent concerns: acute onset, progression over 24 hours, fever, constitutional symptoms, home management attempt, and reassurance-seeking.*

Viral Syndrome: Clinical presentation consistent with viral syndrome: mild tachycardia (HR 113) expected in acute viral illness; normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude bacterial infection or pneumonia, supporting self-limited viral etiology.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and mild cough, and by afternoon she had a fever that got up to 101. She's been pretty fussy and doesn't want to eat much, just wants juice and popsicles. We gave her some children's ibuprofen last night and it helped a little, but she's still not acting like herself today. I'm mainly worried about the fever and want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Viral Syndrome: History and exam consistent w/ viral syndrome: vital signs stable (HR 93 appropriate for age), HEENT exam unremarkable (no focal infection), lungs clear bilaterally (no respiratory involvement). Clinical course and benign exam findings support self-limited viral etiology rather than bacterial infection or systemic disease.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and was kind of fussy, then by afternoon her temperature was 100.5. She's been sneezy and has a lot of clear mucus coming out of her nose, but no cough yet. We gave her some children's pain reliever and she's been drinking okay, but she's not really interested in eating much. I'm mainly worried it's going to turn into something worse or that she'll get an ear infection like she did last month."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: fever, clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, and mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate support upper respiratory involvement; clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 220mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 150mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 4 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and seemed a little fussy, but I thought it was just allergies. Then last night she felt warm, so I took her temperature and it was 100.8. She's had a stuffy nose all day today and won't eat much, just wants to drink. I gave her some children's pain reliever this morning and tried a humidifier, but the fever came back. I'm mostly worried because she's never had a fever this high before and I want to make sure it's not something serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral rhinosinusitis; low-grade fever (100.8°F) and mild tachycardia (HR 119) are expected systemic response in 3 yo; clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 190mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 130mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has been pretty fussy ever since. The drainage started clear but now it's getting thicker, and she's been sneezing a lot. We gave her some children's cold medicine and tried a humidifier last night, but she's still not feeling great and barely touched her breakfast this morning. We're mainly worried it might turn into an ear infection like she had last month."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation, while clear lungs and normal respiratory exam exclude lower respiratory involvement. Mild tachycardia (HR 114) is expected compensatory response to viral illness in 3 yo.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 190mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 130mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She woke up yesterday morning pulling at her right ear and was really fussy. By afternoon she had a fever and wouldn't eat much, just wanted to drink. We gave her some ibuprofen and it helped a little, but she's been inconsolable on and off. She's been tugging at that ear constantly and I'm worried it's an ear infection—her cousin just had one and it sounds the same."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with AOM: fever, erythematous and bulging TM with loss of light reflex, decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy, and MEE all meet diagnostic criteria for acute suppurative OM in this 3 yo.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1150mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1150mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 130mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain

"She woke up yesterday morning with a fever and has been really fussy and clingy all day. She's complaining that her head and muscles hurt, won't eat much, and just wants to lay on the couch. We gave her some children's ibuprofen and she seemed a little better for a bit, but the fever keeps coming back. We're worried because she's never been this sick before and we heard there's flu going around at daycare."

Influenza: Clinical presentation consistent w/ influenza: viral URI sx in 3 yo w/ normal cardiopulmonary exam (HR 96, lungs clear bilaterally) supports uncomplicated influenza w/o evidence of secondary bacterial infection or pneumonia.
- [other] Supportive care with rest and hydration - [follow-up] Return if symptoms worsen or do not improve in 3-5 days
Well-child examination - 3 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started getting a runny nose two days ago, and yesterday it got worse. She's been pretty fussy and won't eat much, just wants her bottle. We've been using the saline drops and a humidifier at night, but the runny nose just keeps going. I'm mainly worried because she's not sleeping well and I want to make sure it doesn't turn into anything worse."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and mild nasal mucosa erythema indicate viral rhinosinusitis, while clear lungs and normal vital signs (HR 99, age-appropriate) exclude lower respiratory involvement and systemic toxicity.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 170mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

History of Present Illness - Parent Narrative: Mila woke up fussy yesterday morning and has been running a fever since then. She's been pulling at her right ear and won't eat much, just wants her bottle. We gave her some children's ibuprofen and it helped a little, but she's still uncomfortable and hasn't slept well last night. We're worried it might be an ear infection and want to make sure she gets better soon.

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are classic for AOM: fever, tachycardia, and tachypnea indicate systemic inflammation; otoscopic findings of erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy confirm middle ear infection with effusion. Absence of pulmonary findings excludes concurrent pneumonia.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 1050mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 1050mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 120mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She's been really fussy since yesterday morning and running a fever. Last night she wouldn't eat much and kept tugging at her right ear, so we knew something was wrong. We gave her some children's ibuprofen and that helped a little, but she's still not herself today and the fever came back. We're worried it might be an ear infection since she had one a few months ago."

Acute Otitis Media: History and exam findings are consistent with acute otitis media: erythematous, bulging TM with loss of light reflex and decreased mobility on pneumatic otoscopy indicate acute inflammation and middle ear effusion. Fever (101.3°F) and tachycardia (HR 125) support acute infection without concurrent respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Amoxicillin 975mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Amoxicillin-Clavulanate 975mg divided BID x 10 days - [medication] Ibuprofen 110mg Q6H PRN PRN - [education] Complete full course of antibiotics - [education] Ibuprofen or acetaminophen for pain control - [education] Return if no improvement in 48-72 hours - [education] Return immediately for high fever, facial swelling, or severe pain
Well-child examination - 2 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started getting a runny nose yesterday morning, and today it's gotten worse. She's been pretty fussy and won't eat much—just wants her milk. We've been wiping her nose a lot and gave her some saline drops, but she still sounds really congested. We're mainly worried because she hasn't been herself and we want to make sure it's not something serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with viral URI: clear rhinorrhea with erythematous nasal mucosa, mild oropharyngeal erythema without exudate, and clear lung fields support upper respiratory involvement without lower respiratory tract disease or bacterial superinfection. Mild tachycardia (HR 116) and tachypnea (RR 25) are expected compensatory responses to viral illness in this age group.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 150mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 100mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

"She started throwing up yesterday morning after breakfast, and then had diarrhea a few times throughout the day. She's been really fussy and won't eat anything—she'll only drink a little bit of water. We gave her some crackers and bananas like we normally do when she has a stomach bug, but she just threw those up too. We're mainly worried because she's been in diapers all day and we want to make sure she's not getting dehydrated."

Viral Gastroenteritis: Clinical presentation consistent w/ viral gastroenteritis: tachycardia (HR 127) and tachypnea (RR 27) suggest compensatory response to likely dehydration from GI losses, while normal HEENT and clear lungs exclude alternative diagnoses (meningitis, pneumonia, other systemic illness).

- [medication] Ondansetron 2mg x1 dose PRN PRN - [education] Oral rehydration with small frequent amounts (1-2 tsp every 5 minutes) - [education] Use Pedialyte or similar oral rehydration solution - [education] Advance diet as tolerated - no need for BRAT restriction - [education] Return if: unable to keep fluids down, no urine in 8 hours, blood in stool, or appears very ill
Well-child examination - 1 year 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Scald burn to hand

"She woke up yesterday morning with a runny nose and has had a fever since this afternoon—we checked it a few times and it's been around 101 or 102. She's been pretty fussy and doesn't want to eat much, just wants to drink her bottle. We gave her some children's pain reliever this morning and tried a humidifier last night, but the fever keeps coming back. We're mostly worried because she's never had a fever this high before and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: fever, clear rhinorrhea, and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation; tachycardia and tachypnea are expected physiologic responses to fever and viral illness in this age group, with clear lung fields excluding lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 140mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 95mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms

Patient: Mila, 1 year 5 months old Chief Complaint: Conjunctivitis HPI (Parent Narrative): "Her left eye started getting red and goopy yesterday morning. Today it's worse—there's yellowish discharge and she keeps rubbing it. We've been wiping it with a warm washcloth, but it's not getting better. I'm worried it might be infected and want to make sure she doesn't get it in the other eye too." --- *This narrative captures typical parent observations: timeline, specific symptoms, attempted home care, and chief concern—all in natural, conversational language appropriate for clinical documentation.*

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings consistent w/ viral conjunctivitis: bilateral conjunctival injection w/ discharge, preauricular lymphadenopathy (classic viral pattern), clear corneas, and normal VA. Mild tachycardia/tachypnea likely reflects systemic viral illness; clear lungs and otherwise benign exam support isolated conjunctivitis w/o lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 1 year 3 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 1 year old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

HPI: Mila's right eye started getting red and goopy yesterday morning. Today both eyes are pretty swollen and crusty, especially when she wakes up. We've been wiping them with a warm washcloth and she seems uncomfortable—rubbing at them a lot and more fussy than usual. We're worried it might be an infection and want to make sure she doesn't need antibiotics.

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings of conjunctival injection with purulent discharge in a 10 mo F are consistent w/ acute conjunctivitis. Tachycardia (HR 117) and tachypnea (RR 32) likely reflect systemic response to infection, while normal cornea and clear lungs help exclude more serious etiologies (keratitis, pneumonia).

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 9 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She's had a runny nose for about 2 days now. At first it was just clear stuff coming out, but yesterday she got really fussy and didn't want to eat much. We've been using the bulb syringe to clean her nose and gave her some extra fluids, but she's still pretty congested. I'm mainly worried because she's not acting like herself and I want to make sure it's not turning into something worse."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral rhinosinusitis; tachycardia (HR 116) and tachypnea (RR 34) reflect systemic response to infection, though clear lung fields exclude lower respiratory involvement at this time.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 110mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [medication] Ibuprofen 70mg Q6-8H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 6 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She woke up yesterday morning with some red bumps on her chest and back. They've been spreading throughout the day and now they're all over her body, even on her face. She's been more fussy than usual and doesn't want to eat as much, but she hasn't had a fever that we know of. We're worried it might be something contagious since her older brother was sick last week."

Rash: Clinical presentation of rash in 5 mo F with vital sign abnormalities (HR 113, RR 28) suggests possible systemic etiology; however, normal HEENT and clear lungs on exam help exclude serious infectious causes (meningitis, pneumonia) and support benign dermatologic process.
- [other] Topical care as appropriate for rash type - [education] Return if rash spreads, becomes painful, or child develops fever

"Her left eye started getting goopy and red yesterday morning. At first I thought it was just sleep in her eye, but it kept draining yellow stuff throughout the day and got more swollen. I've been wiping it with a warm cloth and some saline drops, but it's not getting better. Now her right eye looks like it's starting too, and I'm worried it's an infection."

Conjunctivitis: History and exam findings of conjunctival injection, purulent discharge, and eyelid edema are consistent with acute conjunctivitis. Preauricular lymphadenopathy suggests viral etiology, while clear lungs and normal cornea exclude serious complications such as keratitis or systemic infection.

- [medication] Erythromycin Ophthalmic Ointment per package Apply 0.5 inch ribbon QID x 7 days - [education] Antibiotic drops for bacterial; supportive care for viral - [education] Good hand hygiene to prevent spread - [education] Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup - [education] May return to school 24 hours after starting treatment if bacterial - [education] Return if: vision changes, severe pain, or photophobia
Well-child examination - 4 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Well-child examination - 2 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

"She started getting a runny nose yesterday morning, and it's been pretty constant since then. We noticed she's been sneezing a lot and seems a bit more fussy than usual, especially when trying to feed. We've been using the bulb syringe to clear her nose, but it doesn't seem to help much. We're mainly worried because she's not eating as well as normal, and we want to make sure it's nothing serious."

Upper Respiratory Infection: Clinical presentation consistent with URI: clear rhinorrhea and erythematous nasal mucosa indicate viral upper airway inflammation, while clear lung fields and vital signs (HR 132, RR 28) reflect expected physiologic response in 2 mo-old without lower respiratory involvement.

- [medication] Acetaminophen 60mg Q4-6H PRN PRN - [education] Supportive care with rest and increased fluids - [education] Saline nasal drops and bulb suction for congestion - [education] Honey (if >1 year) for cough relief - [education] Return if: difficulty breathing, fever >3 days, ear pain, or worsening symptoms
Well-child examination - 1 month old
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit
Healthy newborn
- [education] Anticipatory guidance provided - [follow-up] Return for next well-child visit

tr.td: No developmental concerns at this time
No developmental concerns at this time
